phase1.explanation:
Serialization saves objects to a file and restores them later:

Serializable interface:
• Marker interface (no methods)
• Tells Java: "this object can be saved"

Key concepts:
• serialVersionUID — version identifier (controls compatibility)
• transient — fields NOT serialized (sensitive data)
• static fields — NOT serialized (belong to class)

ObjectOutputStream:
• writeObject(obj) — serializes an object
• Can serialize any Serializable object
• Supports primitive types too

ObjectInputStream:
• readObject() — deserializes an object
• Returns Object (needs casting)

What gets serialized:
• Non-transient instance fields
• Primitives and Serializable objects

What doesn't get serialized:
• transient fields
• static fields
• Non-Serializable objects

Best practices:
• Always define serialVersionUID
• Mark sensitive data as transient
• Consider security implications

When to use serialization:
• Save game state
• Cache objects
• Send objects over network
• Store user sessions

phase2.errorMessage:
Exception in thread "main" java.io.NotSerializableException: Person
        at java.base/java.io.ObjectOutputStream.writeObject0(ObjectOutputStream.java:1185)
        at java.base/java.io.ObjectOutputStream.writeObject(ObjectOutputStream.java:349)
        at Test.main(Test.java:16)

phase3.prompt:
Create a Student class that:
• Implements Serializable
• Has fields: name, id, gpa
• Has a transient field: password
• Has serialVersionUID = 1L

In main:
1. Create a Student object
2. Serialize it to "student.ser"
3. Deserialize it back
4. Print both the original and restored objects

Note: The transient field should be null after deserialization.
dialogueHints: Add private static final long serialVersionUID = 1L;
phase3.openingDialogue: Serialization — freeze objects and bring them back! ❄️
